title: The Diaconate: An Office of Unity
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: The Diaconate_ An Office of Unity - Pastor Tito Lyro - 1 Timothy 3_8-13.txt
outline: The reason for the office: Christ as the model servant (John 13)
The reason for the office: unity in the early church (Acts 6)
What the office of deacon is not
The qualifications for the diaconate (1 Timothy 3:8-13)
The role of the deacon's wife (v. 11)
summary: Pastor Tito Lyro preaches on 1 Timothy 3:8-13 in the context of appointing a deacon intern, exploring the biblical origins and purpose of the diaconate. He shows that deacons exist to promote unity in the church, free elders for prayer and the ministry of the Word, and tangibly demonstrate the mercy and service of Christ in the congregation. The office is grounded in Christ's own example of servant leadership at the Last Supper.
transcript:
James tells us that every good gift and every perfect gift is from above, and comes down from the Father of lights. The office of the deacon is one of those good and perfect gifts that the Lord has given to his church in order for her to grow into the fullness of her head, the Lord Jesus Christ. Deacons are chosen from the membership of the church, led by the members of the church, and confirmed to the office by the elders of the church.

The choosing of any officer in the church, whether an elder or a deacon, is a serious and solemn occasion, and the church needs to prepare itself accordingly. Because of the importance of occasions like the choosing of deacons, we have developed a process here at the church that allows everyone involved in this decision to be thoroughly informed.

The process is based on 1 Timothy 3:10, where Paul says, "But let these also first be tested; then let them serve as deacons, being found blameless." So here is the process. The session appoints a man to serve as a deacon intern for no less than three months and no more than six months. During those months, the man serves like an ordained deacon in every capacity, except that he has no decision-making power in the diaconal meetings. The church then has the opportunity to see the man functioning as a deacon. The session — the elders of the church — can evaluate whether the man is fit for the office, and the man himself is able to make his calling to the office sure.

So we have appointed Jim Weston to be a deacon intern of this church for the next few months. We ask that you carefully observe him as he serves you in the church and encourage him as he serves you. I want you to listen to this sermon very attentively so that you know what Christ wants you to do — so that you know what to look for, so that you know how you can serve Jim and be served by Jim and the other deacons as you consider this decision. If everything goes well and Jim is nominated to the office, then the election will be in January, at a regular stated congregational meeting.

Another element of this process is that we don't make an announcement until we have actually interviewed the wife of the man, to make sure she's on board. As we're going to see, there are requirements for the wife as well as for the man. It's interesting that there are no requirements given explicitly for the wife of the elder, but there are explicit requirements for the wife of the deacon — and we're going to see why. Elder Scott Hollander and Elder Andrew Hoy interviewed Tammy, and she is on board with Jim being put in this capacity. We also see her as a fit wife to serve alongside Jim in this capacity as well.

When November or December comes, about a month before the congregational meeting, we will provide an opportunity for the congregation to interview Jim together. That is the final step — for you to hear from him and to determine whether you want him to serve you as a deacon.

## The Reason for the Office: Christ as the Model Servant (John 13)

The passage before us this morning is the fullest description of the kind of man who should be ordained to the office. By giving us the characteristics of the man, Paul implicitly gives us a description of the job of the deacon. Notice that when we read through it, they are all moral characteristics. They are not a set of skills. They are moral, godly characteristics that the man must have.

We're going to look at this passage, and we're going to do it briefly because it is a well-known passage. But before we get there, I want to look at two other passages that set the table for us to examine 1 Timothy 3:8–13 in its fullness. These two passages are going to explain to us the reason for the office of the deacon.

You notice that the title of the sermon is "The Diaconate: An Office of Unity." I say that because God instituted deacons in order that the church might reflect the unity of the Spirit in the bond of love. We think of deacons as the servants of the church — and they are — but primarily the deacons are agents of unity in the church.

We see the reason for the office of the deacon in the ministry of the Lord Jesus Christ himself. There is one particular passage that encapsulates the office of the deacon as seen in Christ — Christ as the deacon, Christ as the servant who ministered at his disciples' feet. That is John 13:1–20. This is a well-known passage — the washing of the disciples' feet on the night Jesus was betrayed.

It is Thursday night. He is going to be crucified on Friday. The worst day in all of human history is going to happen tomorrow. Tonight, our Lord and his disciples make their way from Bethany to Jerusalem to the upper room, where they are going to celebrate the last Passover of the Old Testament and the first Lord's Supper of the New Covenant.

It was a long and dusty road. When they arrived at the upper room, it would have been customary for the host to provide a servant to wash the guests' feet. It was an open-sewer time — dirt roads, sandaled feet — and they were going to recline at table together, so it made practical sense. But it was the lowest of the servants who would do that. The household servants had different ranks, and the lowest of them would come and wash the disciples' feet.

But when they arrived at the upper room, there was no servant there. One can imagine the disciples looking nervously at one another — is Matthew going to do it? Is John going to do it? Is Thaddaeus — just by the name he probably should be the one doing it — is Peter going to do it? Bartholomew, James, the other James, the third James — who is going to wash the disciples' feet?

While they were trying to figure things out, Jesus took his outer clothes off and wrapped a long towel around his waist in the manner of a slave. Then he knelt down, and one by one he began to wash the dirty feet of the people who John had already told us in the Gospel were going to abandon him just a few hours from that moment. Even the feet of the one who was going to leave that place immediately after getting his feet washed to betray him and condemn him to the death of a sinner.

After he finished washing the disciples' feet, John tells us that Jesus looked at the disciples and said the following:

> *"A new commandment I give to you, that you love one another: just as I have loved you, you also are to love one another. By this all people will know that you are my disciples, if you have love for one another."* (John 13:34–35)

Jesus was saying to his disciples that the way he had served them all his life — in that symbolic act that night, and certainly in what he was going to do the next day in dying for their sins — that was an example to them as to how they were to love one another tangibly. As he had served them tangibly, they were to serve one another.

And this is very important because the Gospel tells us that the disciples were having a heated conversation on the way to the upper room that night. As they were coming to celebrate the last Passover of the old covenant and the first Lord's Supper of the new covenant, they were arguing with one another as they walked. They weren't debating the five points of Calvinism. It wasn't about the order of the decrees of God. It wasn't about covenant baptism. They were arguing about which one of them was going to be the greatest in the kingdom of God.

So Jesus washes their feet and says: instead of figuring out who is going to be the greatest, just serve one another as the lowest of the slaves. I, who am your master, can stoop down to serve you as the lowest of the slaves. Can you do the same?

Now, God instituted deacons to incarnate the example of Christ in tangibly and concretely loving and serving the congregation in our midst. Deacons are given to us as a physical reminder of the ministry of service of Christ in the body of Christ. Deacons are to embody Christ's own service of his people, especially as they administer mercy in the life of the local congregation.

## The Reason for the Office: Unity in the Early Church (Acts 6)

Let me prove that to you. Turn to Acts chapter 6, verses 1 through 7. It says this:

> *"Now in those days, when the number of the disciples was multiplying, there arose a complaint against the Hebrews by the Hellenists, because their widows were neglected in the daily distribution. Then the twelve summoned the multitude of the disciples and said, 'It is not desirable that we should leave the word of God and serve tables.' Therefore, brethren, seek out among you seven men of good reputation, full of the Holy Spirit and wisdom, whom we may appoint over this business; but we will give ourselves continually to prayer and to the ministry of the word.' And the saying pleased the whole multitude. And they chose Stephen, a man full of faith and the Holy Spirit, and Philip, Prochorus, Nicanor, Timon, Parmenas, and Nicolas, a proselyte from Antioch, whom they set before the apostles; and when they had prayed, they laid hands on them."* (Acts 6:1–6)

Note that the phrase "serve tables" — that verb for serving tables — is the verb from which we get the noun "deacon."

There was a problem in the church in Jerusalem. Good things were happening — the church was growing and was very active in caring for people in need, especially widows. The godly Jewish people among the early Christians were very concerned about widows and orphans and those in need who were part of the believing community. And the Christian church in Jerusalem was apparently doing a good job of it — but a controversy arose.

There were widows in the church who spoke Aramaic or Hebrew, and there were other widows who spoke Greek. The Greek-speaking Jewish Christian widows thought that perhaps the Hebrew-speaking Jewish Christian widows were getting more of the food or more of the money than they were. So a complaint arose and was brought to the attention of the leadership.

When the controversy was brought to the apostles — who were effectively the elders at the time — they did something very interesting. They didn't say, "We are so committed to the ministry of the word and prayer that we are just going to forget this mercy ministry business." They didn't say that. But they also didn't say, "The mercy ministry to the widows is so important that we are going to forget all this praying and teaching and reading our Bibles and leading the congregation in discipleship and the word." Instead, the apostles, under the inspiration of the Holy Spirit, said: "This is serious business. We're going to appoint deacons."

Acts 6:1–6 is the appointment of the first deacons in the history of the Church of Jesus Christ. And the reason for establishing this office was so that Christ's love could be tangibly displayed in the congregation. The elders cannot fail to devote themselves to the shepherding work of teaching, praying, and leading the congregation in discipleship. So the apostles said to the people: here are the qualifications. These men are going to lead in the mercy ministry of our congregation. Now pick some men who meet those qualifications. The congregation looked at the qualifications, looked at the men, and said: we have seven men who meet those qualifications. So the apostles appointed those men as the first deacons of the church.

This was the church about which Jesus had told the disciples that their witness to the world would depend on the way they love and serve one another.

### Three Reasons Deacons Were Given to the Church

So what is the reason for deacons? Threefold.

First, deacons exist so that the ministry of the word and prayer can flourish under the direction of the elders, and the elders can devote themselves to that. The diaconate was specifically created in Acts 6 so that the elders could pray and teach.

Second, and equally important: the deacons exist to promote unity in the church. Why were they created? Because there was division. And the apostles tasked them with bringing unity. Where there is division, deacons bring unity.

Third, deacons exist so that the ministry of mercy — tangible love — can flourish in the local congregation, under the leadership, exhortation, and example of the deacons.

So deacons are given to the church as a gift so that the church will love in both word and deed — so that truth will be ministered in the congregation and so that mercy will be ministered in the congregation. The gospel ministry is a ministry of word and deed, as John tells us in 1 John 3:18, and neither must be neglected. It is for the church's well-being to have two classes of officers devoted to fostering both aspects of the ministry of the church.

The deacon's work is to complement the elders' ministry of the word and prayer. The deacon is to lead in the local congregation's ministry of mercy to those who are in need in the church. It is an office of service and deed. It embodies the example of Christ. And in the diaconate and the eldership, you have the whole Christ, as it were, exemplified in ministry of word and deed. Isn't our Savior so merciful that he gives us these two offices that show us the fullness of Christ?

## What the Office of the Deacon Is Not

When we are saying what something is, it is always good to also say what it is not. So let me say a few things about what the office of the deacon is not.

Start with the obvious. A deacon is not an elder. A deacon is a deacon. There are two offices in the church, and the church is to have a plurality of elders — and the church is supposed to have a plurality of deacons, a multiplicity of deacons as well.

The deacons are not just administrators. They are in charge of the operational and maintenance aspects of the church budget, and they are in charge of the material property of the church and making repairs. But they only do those things because they love you.

Deacons are not social service directors. Some think that deacons are to get out in the community. But in the New Testament, the focus of the diaconate is always on leading the church in ministry to the congregation of Christians and to other Christians in need — not setting up some sort of community social service program.

So: a deacon is not an elder; a deacon is not just an administrator; and a deacon is not a social worker for the community.

## The Qualifications for the Diaconate (1 Timothy 3:8–13)

Now let's go back to 1 Timothy 3. God wants men for the office of deacon who want to show the love of Christ by serving his body concretely and tangibly. God wants men who are after the reward that comes from Christ, not from other men. In verse 13 of 1 Timothy 3, Paul says:

> *"For those who have served well as deacons obtain for themselves a good standing and great boldness in the faith which is in Christ Jesus."* (1 Timothy 3:13)

Deacons are men who are seeking the glory of Christ and are seeking to hear from the Lord, "Well done, my good and faithful servant." The office of deacon is emphatically an office of servants. The deacon is not out for power or prestige. He is a man who wants to serve. He wants to help when the people of God are hurting and aid them. When the people of God are in need, he wants to comfort and assist them. He wants to make the Christian claims of love very tangible to those who are suffering.

Paul doesn't describe the office in any kind of great detail in 1 Timothy 3. But in Acts 6, and in 1 Corinthians 12:27–28, where Paul talks about the gift of helping and the gift of ministry, and in Romans 12:6–7, where he talks about the gift of service — Paul indicates that the office of deacon is not the office of the elder in training. It is not the office of janitor. It is not the office of "I hope one day to become an elder." The office of service in the church is a goal unto itself. It is a high calling. It is not a lesser calling than any other calling. It is a high calling in the Church of Jesus Christ, very important and necessary for the well-being of the body of Christ.

Very often men are elected to the diaconate who are never elected elders. And that is fine. That doesn't mean they haven't made the grade or moved up the ranks. The two offices are distinct, and some are given the gifts and desire and calling for one office, and some for the other.

So we want men in the diaconate who exude the desire to serve the flock — and we see that very clearly in Jim. That is why we have appointed him. We want men who love serving the Lord's people in time of need.

### The Five Categories of Qualification

God wants men who are godly and self-controlled, qualified by their desire to serve, their faith, and their proven character. We see that in verses 8 to 10 and 12 to 13. And notice that just like the elders, Paul's qualifications are primarily moral. They must have the desire to serve. They must have the character of a man who is going to minister faithfully in the Lord's church.

Notice several parallels. The diaconate, just like the elders, must be godly — spiritually and in the family. It is vital that if they are going to spiritually serve the congregation, they know how to spiritually serve their own families. Notice also that they are to be tested, in verse 10 — and that is why we have this internship process.

The moral qualifications for the office fall into five categories.

**First**, the deacon is to have self-control in speech. A deacon is going to find out things about the lives of families in the church that could be hurtful and divisive to the fellowship if shared with others. He has to be a man who can keep his lips tight, even when he finds out about a great deal going on in the lives of needy people in the congregation. He needs to be a person who is trustworthy, one who has self-control in his tongue.

**Second**, he has self-control in the area of drinking. This is a person who manifests self-control with regard to alcohol and is not getting high on drugs either. By doing that, he inspires confidence so that when money is entrusted to him, it will not be misused.

**Third**, he is not to be fond of gaining money unlawfully or unethically. He has self-control in the area of money. He is in charge of taking all the tithes and gifts that you give and making sure that they are used for the glory of God, so he needs to be a man that you can trust with money.

**Fourth**, he is to be faithful in all things — a man of proven character in every area of life.

**Fifth**, he is orthodox in his beliefs. In verse 9 Paul says, "holding the mystery of the faith with a pure conscience." Deacons are not required to be able to teach as the elders are, but they are required to hold to historic, faithful, biblical, Christian orthodoxy with a clear conscience. You are going to have the opportunity to examine Jim at some point to see whether he holds to those things. We have already done so, and we believe that he does.

God wants mercy to be tangibly ministered by people who really believe the Word. So often, in the last hundred years or so, we have seen that the people most interested in mercy ministry tend to be the least interested in doctrine. But God wants the people who are serving a local congregation to be sound and committed theologically.

So God wants men who have upstanding moral character — men of dignity, husbands of one wife, good managers of their children and household. The fundamental requirement for the office is that the deacon requires godliness.

Notice also that these requirements are not super-Christian requirements, but what every man in the church should be. If you look at these requirements as a man and think, "I can't be a deacon because I don't really meet these requirements" — it is time to repent and grow in the Lord, because these are floor requirements. They are not the ceiling we are all aspiring to reach. This is where we all should be to begin with. This is the description of the Christian man, and it should be the description of every man in this room.

## The Role of the Deacon's Wife (v. 11)

Now look at verse 11. Verse 11 says, "Likewise, their wives must be reverent, not slanderous, temperate, faithful in all things."

The original Greek language does not make a differentiation between "wife" and "woman" — it is the same word, and context dictates. Here you can see that some translations use "wives" and others use "women." There have been three interpretations offered for this verse: that these are the wives of the deacons; that these are female deacons or deaconesses; or that these are women who serve alongside the deacons but are not ordained to the office.

Because a woman could not meet some of the qualifications for the office — such as being "the husband of one wife" — no matter what culture says, a woman cannot be the husband of one wife. So we must set aside the interpretation that this verse opens the office to women. God calls men to be deacons.

Because of the proximity with the previous verse, and because verse 12 continues to describe the male deacon, the best interpretation is that verse 11 is referring to the wife of the deacon, if he is married. The reason it appears here is that the nature of the work of the deacon is such that it will require the help of women in ministry to the other people in the church. This is an invitation to and an extension of women's involvement in the diaconal ministry — not as an officer, but as a helper.

Some of the women in the church have experienced that, where I've called and said, "This person needs some ministry — could you take care of that?" And most of the time they say yes, willingly and eagerly. We need women involved in the diaconal ministry who are not ordained as deacons — rightly so, because the Bible says men should be deacons.

## The Reward of Faithful Service (v. 13)

Lastly, God wants men who serve so that they can hear the "well done" of Jesus — see verse 13. And this is for Jim: the only reward offered to you as a deacon is the approval of the Lord. I will say that those who serve in this often quiet, behind-the-scenes work of deacons will be rewarded with high standing. Though they may be less in the eyes of the world, and even in the eyes of some in the church, they will be made first.

It is interesting that there is no blessing explicitly associated with the office of the elder at the end of the qualifications. But at the end of the qualifications for the office of the deacon, they will hear, "Well done, my good and faithful servant" from their master. They will have greater assurance, encouragement, boldness, and freedom.

We see this pattern happening all the time in the Christian church. Those who most give themselves away and most die to their own selves are the ones who live with the most freedom, joy, satisfaction, and fulfillment. And that is part of the office of the deacon. Deacons give themselves away in washing the feet of the brethren, serving them in mercy ministries, and they attain the highest standing and great confidence in the Lord. They are also an example to us, because the deacon does by duty what all of us ought to do by love.

Notice how Luke ends the passage about the first deacons in Acts 6:7:

> *"Then the word of God spread, and the number of the disciples multiplied greatly in Jerusalem, and a great many of the priests were obedient to the faith."* (Acts 6:7)

And why? Because deacons were appointed to serve in the church. The church is blessed. The gospel goes forth. Souls are saved as deacons serve the Lord in the local church. May it be true of our church and of our deacons.
